persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter-hypothesis is a same-name relative (e.g. a son 'James Robert Longden') serving in Ceylon in the same years, or that the bio's single middle initial 'R.' conceals a different middle name than 'Robert'. This fails: Longden is a rare surname in the corpus (8 officials, 3 biographies), the bio explicitly states 'gov., Ceylon, 1877 to 1883' which brackets the stint's listed years 1878-1883 exactly, the stint position 'Governor, &c.' is the same office, and both carry the K.C.M.G. (bio: 1876; staff list: K.C.M.G. shown 1878-1880, consistent with an 1876 award and with the G.C.M.G. arriving only in 1883 at the end of the tenure). A second knighted J. R. Longden governing the same colony over the same six-year span is not a realistic alternative. The absence of edition co-occurrence is expected since the stint editions (1878-1880, 1883 staff lists) largely predate the surviving biography sections (1883 onward).
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second Sir James Robert Longden, K.C.M.G., alive in 1878-1883 (e.g. a son with identical name and honour), or records showing the Ceylon governor of 1878-1883 had different forenames or a different knighthood date.

notes: Single bio, single stint, rare surname. Multiple independent agreeing dimensions: exact colony + bracketing dates (1877-1883 vs listed 1878-1883), exact office (governor), forename/initials agreement (James R. vs James Robert), and shared K.C.M.G. with chronologically consistent award year. No live counter-hypothesis.
